key_insight: A lightweight router can adapt when and how strongly Hebbian fast memory is used in Vision Transformers, improving few-shot recognition over fixed memory behavior.
engineering_relevance: This suggests a practical way to add episode-specific plasticity to transformer backbones with minimal overhead, useful for adaptive representation learning under limited labeled data.
method: MLP router controls memory activation, update strength, and memory retention
Three variants are studied: Adaptive Placement, Adaptive Plasticity, and Fully Adaptive Hebbian Routing
Applied to ViT-Small, DeiT-Small, and Swin-Tiny for few-shot classification
evidence_signal: Evaluations on 5-way 1-shot Omniglot, CIFAR-FS, and cross-domain CIFAR-FS→Omniglot show consistent gains, with Swin-Tiny improving from 96.74% fixed Hebbian to 96.94% fully adaptive and latency dropping from 16.51 ms to 14.05 ms.
watch_item: The router adds extra learned control logic, so benefits may depend on backbone choice, task domain, and careful tuning of memory placement and plasticity.
paper_title: Adaptive Hebbian Memory Routing in Vision Transformers for Few-Shot Learning
